---
title: "adding to startup applications? 12.10"
date: 2012-12-28
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-28
I recently installed a clock app on my laptop, I want to add the executable file to my "startup applications" list but I have no idea where to find the file. can someone tell me how? I am kinda lost on where to look .

---

### Post by gnush on 2012-12-28
What's the application called? My first suggestion would be to look in the */usr/bin* directory.

Or, you can just perform a system-wide search.

```
# find / | grep -i *filename*
```

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-28
> **gnush said:**
> What's the application called? My first suggestion would be to look in the */usr/bin* directory.

Or, you can just perform a system-wide search.

```
# find / | grep -i *filename*
```

The app is named "clock" i downloaded and installed from omgubuntu.com
  I looked in /usr/bin for the clock file/app all i found was a python text doc. I tryed adding that to startapps and nothing. then i tried the search string you posted and it didnt work. everytime i tried it it just returned to the cursor.

anyidea what the executable  file extention would be? I am used toi looking for exe files in windows .

---

### Post by Hadaka on 2012-12-28
Hi, i think you got just the cursor because you included the "#" in the
command....or ..clock is not the correct filename.  Try this...

```
find / | grep -i clock 
```if you have a file named clock...that should find it for you along with
any other file with the word "clock" in it.....so look at the entire output,
more than likely the file is in the Downloads directory.....cd /Downloads    then....    ls
hope this helps.

---

### Post by howefield on 2012-12-28
/usr/bin/clock

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-28
> **howefield said:**
> /usr/bin/clock
tried that got nothing . i also tried the clock file in usr/share/applications also nothing.

> Hi, i think you got just the cursor because you included the "#" in the
command....or ..clock is not the correct filename.  Try this...

```
find / | grep -i clock 
```if you have a file named clock...that should find it for you along with
any other file with the word "clock" in it.....so look at the entire output,
more than likely the file is in the Downloads directory.....cd /Downloads    then....    ls
hope this helps.
Thanks for the correction the code worked fine , but you would no believe how many files have the word "clock in them " wow.

### Post by NikTh on 2012-12-28
Hi ,
is this the app ? => [http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2012/12/clock-time-app-for-ubuntu](http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2012/12/clock-time-app-for-ubuntu)

**EDIT: **
Just installed it , and runs as [[COLOR=#DD4814]**howefield**[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=186490") suggests. 

Sorry but.. I cannot stop my self from say that .... It is Ugly :P 

So , check if you installed this app correctly.. I noted that some dependencies were missing.
Use 
```
sudo apt-get install -f
``` to fix them.

Thanks

---

### Post by howefield on 2012-12-28
This clock ?

---

### Post by Hadaka on 2012-12-28
Glad that worked for you, if the link NikTh sent is where you got the file.
is should be in Downloads or possibly Desktop....to see do this...
and note the uppercase "D" for both directories.

```
cd /Downloads
ls 
``````
cd Desktop
ls 
```thats lowercase "L"....ls......linux speak for "list"

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-28
> **howefield said:**
> This clock ?
yup thats it, i setup the startup app settings just as they are on your display and it will not auto load at startup.

---

### Post by howefield on 2012-12-28
> **Hadaka said:**
> Glad that worked for you, if the link NikTh sent is where you got the file.
is should be in Downloads or possibly Desktop....to see do this...
and note the uppercase "D" for both directories.

Finding the downloaded package file isn't the issue.

It is finding the path to the installed executable so it can be added to the list of applications launched upon boot.

/usr/bin/clock

---

You have rebooted ?

..the sure thing is that fits in Windows 8 (metro style , you know) :P 

Conky is not difficult.. no no .. is very easy  to install and conkyrc files are everywhere and ready for use .. the little tricky part for a new user is IF he/she wants to configure- to change - the conkyrc file..

### Post by NikTh on 2012-12-29
If you run the application from terminal.. is it opens from the first time ? 

Try ```
clock
``` 

or 

```
/usr/bin/clock
``` 

Do you have Ubuntu 12.10 with Unity or some other desktop environment ? 

Thanks

### Post by gnush on 2012-12-29
> **cabz said:**
> /home/cabz/Desktop/Screenshot from 2012-12-29 09:40:07.png[ATTACH]229287[/ATTACH]

If you choose "run in terminal", do you get any messages? If so, what do they say?

Also, you can just open up a terminal and enter the command *clock* and it will execute the program, because it's in the */usr/bin/* directory.

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-29
> **gnush said:**
> If you choose "run in terminal", do you get any messages? If so, what do they say?

Also, you can just open up a terminal and enter the command *clock* and it will execute the program, because it's in the */usr/bin/* directory.
  if i choose run in terminal all i get is a flashing cursor and nothing else , when I typed clock in a term window I got nothing as well the only way it worked was to hit "run twice and then it started . 
I got to install the conky stuff and got the basic conky script running , the n I restarted the pc . the damn clock loaded at start! but only once the next reboot nothing again.
I am stumped .

---

### Post by NikTh on 2012-12-29
Lets see the results 

```
ls -l /usr/bin/clock
```Maybe if we create a script and add a delay option works better ? Who knows ? Lets try 

```
gedit startclock
```add inside
```
#!/bin/bash
sleep 15 
/usr/bin/clock
```save it and then ..
```
chmod +x startclock
```Now add to startup applications this line
```
/home/<your username>/startclock
```See if works .. after 15 seconds from login  clock should be open.. will be there ?

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-29
> **NikTh said:**
> Lets see the results 

```
ls -l /usr/bin/clock
```Maybe if we create a script and add a delay option works better ? Who knows ? Lets try 

```
gedit startclock
```add inside
```
#!/bin/bash
sleep 15 
/usr/bin/clock
```save it and then ..
```
chmod +x startclock
```Now add to startup applications this line
```
/home/<your username>/startclock
```See if works .. after 15 seconds from login  clock should be open.. will be there ?
well that didnt work either, I did find out what might be the problem though. I removed the clock app  and tried a reinstall . when i went to the download page i got this[ATTACH]229316[/ATTACH] than after tring to install the new file i got this .
[ATTACH]229317[/ATTACH] I am thinking it is the app not just me.

---

### Post by mc4man on 2012-12-30
At the least it's a poorly packaged app - from the .deb's control file,
> Depends: python (>= 2.7), python (<< 2.8), python2.7, python-gtk2, python-webkit (>= 1.1.8), [COLOR="Blue"]libwebkitgtk-1.0-0,[/COLOR] [COLOR="Red"]libwebkitgtk-1.0[/COLOR], libjavascriptcoregtk-1.0-0

The same source with 2 version numbers, you'll only have 1 installed/installable, the blue here in 13.04 
He could have picked one or the other or maybe a | between the two would have worked  - (either

(extracted the deb, edited that line to remove the red, repacked to .deb  & installed - seems to start up about 75% of the time in the same place, rather big & soon gone.

---

### Post by cabz on 2012-12-31
Well I contacted the clock apps author and told him about the package issues he revised the app and sent it to me . I reinstalled it and it seems to be working now . He added a timer to the app and i sugjested that he inckude a notification on it so when the timer runs out it lets you know. it still likes to not load all the time but still works better than before.
i'm gonna call this done and play with setting up conky next.

thanks for all the help !):P

---
